title: Inheritance
subtitle: Let one class borrow another's powers so you never write the same code twice.
goal: Use extends to make a subclass reuse a superclass, call the parent constructor with super(...), reuse a parent method with super.method(), override methods with @Override, and understand that every class secretly extends Object and can override toString().

blocks.heading: What is inheritance? 🧬
blocks.html:
Imagine you built a wonderful Animal class with an eat() method. Now you want a Dog that can eat AND bark. Do you copy all the eating code into Dog? No way!

Instead you say: "Dog, you're a kind of Animal — just take everything Animal has." That borrowing is called inheritance. The class you borrow from is the superclass (parent), and the one that borrows is the subclass (child). We connect them with the magic word extends.

blocks.caption: Dog extends Animal and gets eat() for free
blocks.output:
This animal is eating.
Woof! Woof!
blocks.explain: Dog never wrote an eat() method, but it can still call eat() because Dog extends Animal. The child inherits the parent's methods. Dog adds its own bark() on top. That's reuse with zero copy-paste!

blocks.html: The "is-a" test 🧪: Use inheritance only when the child truly is a kind of the parent. A Dog is an Animal ✅. A Car is a Vehicle ✅. But a Car is NOT a kind of Engine — a car has an engine, so that's not inheritance.

blocks.heading: Calling the parent's constructor with super(...) 🏗️
blocks.html:
When the parent class has a constructor that needs information (like a name), the child must pass that info up to the parent. You do it with super(...) — it means "run my parent's constructor first."

The super(...) call must be the very first line inside the child's constructor.

blocks.caption: super(name) hands the name up to Animal
blocks.output:
I am an animal named Rex.
Rex says Woof!
blocks.explain: Dog's constructor calls super(name), which runs Animal's constructor and stores the name. Because of that, Dog can use the inherited name field inside bark() — even though Dog never declared name itself.

blocks.heading: Overriding: the child does it differently ✏️
blocks.html:
Sometimes the child wants to replace a parent method with its own version. That's called overriding. You write a method with the exact same name in the child, and you mark it with @Override so Java double-checks you did it right.

Now when you call that method on a child object, Java runs the child's version instead of the parent's.

blocks.caption: Cat overrides speak()
blocks.explain: Both classes have a speak() method. The plain Animal uses the parent version, but the Cat replaced it with its own. The @Override tag tells Java 'I mean to replace the parent method' — if you misspell the name, Java warns you.

blocks.html: Always add @Override! It's optional, but if you forget and accidentally typo the method name (like speek), Java would silently create a brand-new method instead of overriding. @Override catches that mistake for you.

blocks.heading: super.method() and the secret Object parent 👑
blocks.html:
Overriding doesn't have to throw away the parent's work. You can call super.method() to run the parent's version and then add your own extra steps.

Here's a secret: every Java class extends a built-in class called Object, even if you never write extends. Object gives every class a toString() method that decides what prints when you do System.out.println(myObject). The default is ugly (like Dog@1b6d), so we usually override toString() to print something friendly.

blocks.caption: super.fetch() reuses the parent, and toString() makes printing pretty
blocks.output:
Rex runs to fetch the ball.
Rex brings it back!
Animal[name=Rex]
blocks.explain: Dog's fetch() calls super.fetch() to run Animal's version first, then prints its own extra line. And because Animal overrode toString() (inherited from Object), println(rex) shows the friendly Animal[name=Rex] instead of a messy code.

blocks.question: Which keyword makes one class inherit from another in Java?
blocks.explain: You write 'class Dog extends Animal' to make Dog a subclass of Animal. The keyword is extends.

blocks.question: What does super(name) do inside a subclass constructor?
options: Creates a brand-new variable called super
blocks.explain: super(...) runs the superclass's constructor, passing along the values it needs. It must be the first line inside the child constructor.

blocks.question: Why do we put @Override above a method?
options: It makes the method run faster
It tells Java we mean to replace a parent method, so Java catches typos
It hides the method from the parent
It is required or the code won't compile
blocks.explain: @Override is a safety check. It tells Java 'this should replace a parent method' — if the name doesn't match a real parent method, Java gives you an error instead of silently making a new method.

blocks.title: Start the car
blocks.instructions: Car already extends Vehicle, so it inherited the start() method. Right now main() only honks. Add one line that calls myCar.start() BEFORE the honk, so the output shows the engine starting first.
blocks.solutionOutput:
The engine starts. Vroom!
Beep beep!
blocks.passMsg: Vroom! The Car used a method it inherited from Vehicle without writing it. 🚗
blocks.failMsg: Add the line myCar.start(); before myCar.honk(); — start() comes from the Vehicle parent.

blocks.title: Make the cat meow
blocks.instructions: Cat extends Animal but hasn't overridden makeSound() yet, so it prints the boring generic sound. Add an @Override makeSound() method inside Cat that prints exactly Meow meow! instead.
blocks.passMsg: Purr-fect! The Cat overrode the parent method with its own voice. 🐱
blocks.failMsg: Inside Cat, add: @Override void makeSound() { System.out.println("Meow meow!"); }

challenge.title: A polite dog that remembers its parent
challenge.instructions: Finish two overrides. (1) In Animal, override toString() to return "Animal named " + name. (2) In Dog, override greet() so it first calls super.greet() (the parent's hello) and then prints name + " also says Woof!". The output must match exactly.
challenge.solutionOutput:
Animal named Buddy
Hi, I am Buddy.
Buddy also says Woof!
challenge.hint: toString() goes in Animal and returns (not prints) the text. In Dog's greet(), the first line is super.greet(); then a System.out.println for the Woof line. Mark both with @Override.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Brilliant! You used super to call the parent, overrode toString(), and made a class that's truly an Animal. Day 13 conquered!
